parentQuestion: As sound levels increase in the spiral organ (of Corti), outer hair cells stiffen the basilar membrane.

scaffolds.q: The organ of Corti contains two types of hair cells: inner and outer.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Inner hair cells (IHCs) send signals to the brain; outer hair cells (OHCs) modulate mechanics.

scaffolds.q: Inner hair cells are the primary sensory cells that signal sound to the brain.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! ~95% of auditory nerve fibers connect to inner hair cells.

scaffolds.q: Outer hair cells can actively change their length (contract and elongate).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! OHCs contain prestin protein that makes them 'electromotile' — they move!

scaffolds.q: The basilar membrane vibrates in response to sound waves.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Different frequencies cause maximum vibration at different locations.

scaffolds.q: Outer hair cells can amplify quiet sounds by adding energy to basilar membrane movement.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! OHCs act as 'cochlear amplifiers' boosting sensitivity by ~40-60 dB.

scaffolds.q: Outer hair cells can also DAMPEN loud sounds to protect the inner ear.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! OHC activity compresses the dynamic range — protecting from damage.

scaffolds.q: OHC electromotility can stiffen the basilar membrane in response to loud sounds.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! At high intensities, OHCs modify membrane mechanics to limit movement.

scaffolds.q: Without outer hair cells, hearing becomes less sensitive and less frequency-selective.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! OHC damage causes hearing loss with recruitment (abnormal loudness growth).

scaffolds.q: Outer hair cells stiffen the basilar membrane at high sound levels, providing dynamic range compression.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! OHCs are the cochlea's gain control — amplifying quiet, compressing loud.
